# Conclusion

It outlines a thesis focused on the digital subsystem of a daisy-chainable RGBW LED driver IC idea of aircraft grade. The thesis focuses on a deterministic serial daisy-chain communication architecture, 16-bit PWM generation, and adherence to strict timing constraints to guarantee perceptual simultaneity across lengthy segment chains, all of which are guided by AES's ordered wishlist and internal digital needs. A model-based design methodology is adopted to ensure correctness and feasibility at multiple abstraction levels. System-level validation using MATLAB/Simulink allows early exploration of protocol behavior, latency accumulation, and PWM frequency constraints.Using the GateMate platform and an open-source CAD toolchain (Yosys, nextpnr, openFPGALoader), the work is verified using FPGA prototyping, allowing for transparent evaluation and repeatable builds. Several LLMs are explored by University Chat AI to increase engineering productivity, and accuracy is guaranteed by thorough simulation and hardware testing. The anticipated result is a digital reference architecture that is ready for ASICs, has measured performance, and has a well-defined integration route for a future LED driver IC.
